This is the error I was encountering:

> requests.exceptions.ConnectionError: HTTPConnectionPool(host='x.x.x.x', port=8200): Max retries exceeded with url: /rootDesc.xml (Caused by <class 'socket.error'>: [Errno 54] Connection reset by peer)

I suppose I could change this to catch `requests.exceptions.ConnectionError`, but then if the HHTP request errors out for some other reason (e.g., 4xx or 5xx response code) or the request is fine but there's some kind of XML parse error we'd hit the same undesired behavior.

Generally speaking, I think catching exceptions here broadly is the right thing to do, since we're putting ourselves out there and hoping for a very specific result. Anything unexpected means that we're likely not talking to a Yamaha RXV* and we should just move on. That said, I don't know python all that well, so maybe it's better practice to catch a superclass of request errors and a general XML error instead of just everything?

the old code (not written by me) defaulted to Cuda 8 no matter what unless CUDA_TOOLKIT var is set.
my change makes it so that it defaults to whatever version find_package(CUDA) finds, which is the default behavior in older versions (non FIRST_CUDA versions). this is still overridable by CUDA_TOOLKIT var. if you specify a version that way and CMake can’t find it, you get an error (CMake > 9.0 understands cuda compiling). 
alternately, you can specify the cuda directory which will be picked up by find_package(CUDA). see cmake’s FindCUDA.cmake for details.

I've used codes like 
```python
tp = nd.sum((pred == 1) * (label == 1)).asscalar()
fp = nd.sum((pred == 1) * (label == 0)).asscalar()
fn = nd.sum((pred == 0) * (label == 1)).asscalar()
precision = float(tp) / (tp + fp)
recall = float(tp) / (tp + fn)
f1 = 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall)
```
to calculate the F1 and I find it's much faster in GPU.

the timeout is measured in milliseconds.

The description of what this does is a little inaccurate.  It's probably a good idea to link to http://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/cookies.html#how-cookies-work but summarize it here as:

sync_timeout specifies the number of milliseconds that you want to wait to observe a synchronization cookie.  The synchronization cookie is created at the start of your `clock` call and, once the cookie is observed, means that the clock value returned by this command is at least as current as the time of your `clock` call.

If no sync_timeout is specified, the returned clock value is the instantaneous value of the clock associated with the watched root, and may be almost immediately invalidated if there are any filesystem notifications that are yet to be processed.

I think that description of parameters is misleading / implementation is wrong. From the cutadapt manual::

    -q [5'CUTOFF,]3'CUTOFF, --quality-cutoff=[5'CUTOFF,]3'CUTOFF
    Trim low-quality bases from 5' and/or 3' ends of each read before adapter
    removal. Applied to both reads if data is paired. If one value  is  given,  only
    the  3'  end  is  trimmed.  If  two comma-separated cutoffs are given, the 5'
    end is trimmed with the first cutoff, the 3' end with the second.

The roles of leading / trailing shouod be reversed.

This is a little magical. We're using the [PythonJsonLogger](https://github.com/madzak/python-json-logger) which extends the python logging.formatter which takes an old style format string as an argument to its constructor. 

The PythonJsonLogger keeps this api, but parses the `fmt` argument (which is an old-style format string) to get the set of require keys for the json log that it will generate. 

That means, to ensure we get keys like `levelname` we need to pass a format string that mentions those keys and the PythonJSONLogger will try to convert the old style of specifying require keys (i.e. via a format string) and convert it into keys that it will make sure show up in the json it generates.

One more comment here — when I see the `user_agent` variable later on in the code, I think it's a UserAgent object, but it's actually not because you serialize it here and pass only the string down.

You could pass down the UserAgent object all the way through into Connection, and serialize it in there for a bit more clarity. This also addresses this failure mode: This `str()` call is actually super important because it's the thing that incurs the expensive `import` calls — because you're doing it here, if I were to construct a different `Connection` object and pass it in to Client, Connection would end up either erroring or running the imports every single HTTP request. To fix this, definitely should push the `str()` handling down into Connection, so that Connection handles the serialization of UserAgent no matter what.

Each log message is currently its own emission of spinel message `VALUE_IS(STREAM_DEBUG)` from NCP. But at some point earlier, we were considering to add priority over the NCP spinel messages and possibly let longer log messages to be chopped and sent in chunks. It's not the case in the current code.

That's why wpantund treats the `STREAM_DEBUG` as a stream, and it buffers the received chunks till it sees `\n` and then dumps the log message (or it dumps the log message when it run out of buffer).   

The configuration option of `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_LOG_SUFFIX` was added to give us flexibility to handle all these cases. 

For NCP builds intended for use with wpantund, we use a project specific configuration header file where we define `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_LOG_SUFFIX` as `"\n"`.  

For build where posix logging is used we keep the default settings.

There is a problem with this, it doesn't work if you have some extra args with `-p`, for example `-p "mpv --no-audio"`. However, I have a fix ;)

```diff
diff --git a/src/streamlink_cli/output.py b/src/streamlink_cli/output.py
index 31af016..14fc797 100644
--- a/src/streamlink_cli/output.py
+++ b/src/streamlink_cli/output.py
@@ -82,6 +82,7 @@ class PlayerOutput(Output):
         self.namedpipe = namedpipe
         self.http = http
         self.title = title
+        self.player = self.supported_player(self.cmd)

         if self.namedpipe or self.filename or self.http:
             self.stdin = sys.stdin
@@ -100,6 +101,13 @@ class PlayerOutput(Output):
         sleep(0.5)
         return self.player.poll() is None

+    @staticmethod
+    def supported_player(cmd):
+        """
+        Check if the current player supports adding a title
+
+        :param cmd: cmd to test
+        :return: name of the player|None
+        """
+        for player, possiblecmds in SUPPORTED_PLAYERS.items():
+            for possiblecmd in possiblecmds:
+                if possiblecmd in cmd:
+                    return player
+
     def _create_arguments(self):
         if self.namedpipe:
             filename = self.namedpipe.path
@@ -115,13 +123,13 @@ class PlayerOutput(Output):

         if self.title is not None:
             # vlc
-            if self.cmd in SUPPORTED_PLAYERS['vlc']:
+            if self.player == "vlc":
                 # see https://wiki.videolan.org/Documentation:Format_String/, allow escaping with \$
                 self.title = self.title.replace("$", "$$").replace("\$$", "$")
                 extra_args.extend(["--input-title-format", self.title])

             # mpv
-            if self.cmd in SUPPORTED_PLAYERS['mpv']:
+            if self.player == "mpv":
                 # see https://mpv.io/manual/stable/#property-expansion, allow escaping with \$
                 self.title = self.title.replace("$", "$$").replace("\$$", "$")
                 extra_args.extend(["--title", self.title])    
```
